The piezoelectric effect has two main directions of conversion. Firstly, when an alternating electric field is applied across a piezoelectric material, such as that found in transducer elements used for ultrasound imaging, mechanical vibrations are produced. This means that electrical energy is converted into sound waves or mechanical motion. Secondly, the inverse process occurs during the receiving phase: when high-frequency sound waves from the internal anatomy of the patient hit the transducer probe, they cause the piezoelectric material to generate an alternating voltage, thus converting sound waves back into electrical signals.

This process enables the generation of ultrasonic images by creating high-frequency sound waves that can penetrate tissues and provide detailed internal anatomy information.
  
- The other direction involves the conversion of mechanical vibrations back into an electrical signal. When the transducer probe is exposed to returning echoes, these mechanical vibrations are transformed into an electric signal, which is then processed to create the ultrasound image.

Therefore, the two directions of conversion in the piezoelectric effect as it applies to ultrasound imaging involve converting:

1.  Electrical energy (applied voltage) into mechanical vibration to generate high-frequency sound waves for imaging.
2.  Mechanical vibration from returning echoes back into an electrical signal to process and display the ultrasound image.

These conversions are fundamental to how ultrasound imaging devices work, enabling them to capture detailed images of internal anatomy without the need for ionizing radiation.
